lens.included_activities: Independent title abstract, title-search, settlement, closing, and related real-estate document-preparation offices that sell repeat services to external transaction participants.
lens.excluded_activities: Law offices, captive lender or insurer units, title-insurance underwriters and non-control financing vehicles; one-off internal real-estate administration is outside the lens.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Transaction-based fees paid directly or through closing costs by buyers, sellers, lenders, insurers, and their agents; repeat referral relationships create recurring order flow rather than subscription revenue.

a.bridge: S1 measures tasks and work activities for the title-examiner occupation, not wage-weighted employment in NAICS 541191 or AI substitution. I treat document retrieval, transcription, structured extraction, record updating, and first-pass summarization as exposed, while reserving legal interpretation, exception clearing, and closing coordination. This likely overstates industry-wide exposure because the occupation omits some less-substitutable settlement and relationship work.
a.rationale: The occupation's core work is examining documents, summarizing recorded instruments, maintaining title records, and processing information, which makes a substantial share suitable for AI-assisted extraction and drafting. Accuracy, compliance judgment, and exception resolution prevent treating all of that work as avoidable labor.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-linked NAICS 541191 occupation census plus time-and-motion data from independent agencies, split between search, examination, curative, closing, sales, and administration, would materially move this range.
caveats: O*NET task importance is not a wage share or an automation measurement.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has the stated QCEW-2024/SUSB-2022 vintage mismatch and IPS harmonization limitation.

rho.rationale: Five years is sufficient for first-pass search, intake, document extraction, and workflow-assist deployment, but title work remains accuracy-sensitive and state practice varies. Human review remains important for ambiguous records, underwriting objections, and fraud-sensitive closings.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor-hours, error, claim, and cycle-time data from a representative independent title agency cohort using AI in production, along with underwriter approval rules by state, would materially change implementation expectations.
caveats: This is a forward implementation judgment, not an observed adoption rate.
S5 is an industry-association publication and its statement on human judgment is not an independent measurement.

e.rationale: Most lower-middle-market firms classified here plausibly sell external title, search, or settlement work repeatedly, but the lens excludes captive offices and businesses whose economics are principally underwriting or affiliated lending rather than an outsourced operator service.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS business list matched to title-insurer appointments, lender affiliation, customer concentration, and EBITDA would establish the eligible share directly.
caveats: No public source was found that measures eligibility inside the frozen EBITDA-band firm count.
The frozen firm-count input is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using the provided Damodaran margin and may not identify captive or affiliated units.

s5.rationale: Reported acquisitions demonstrate that transferable title agencies are bought, including agencies with long-tenured leadership, but public announcements do not provide a denominator, owner-age distribution, or a five-year control-transfer rate for eligible lower-middle-market firms. The range therefore remains deliberately broad.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction database covering private title and settlement firms, matched to firm size and ownership age, plus owner succession interviews, would materially narrow the estimate.
caveats: Observed announcements are selected transactions, not a representative sale-rate sample.
The estimate excludes closures and reorganizations that do not preserve transferable operations.

q.rationale: Automation savings can be retained through faster service and lower rework, but closing services are often shoppable, comparison prices are visible, and lenders or referral partners influence provider selection. Those features make material sharing through price, service levels, and competitive response plausible.
q.change_evidence: Agency-level fee schedules, lender and realtor referral contracts, title-underwriter remittance terms, and post-automation gross-margin cohorts would directly measure retained benefit.
caveats: This is not a measured pass-through rate.
Retention differs materially by state, purchase versus refinance mix, underwriter relationship, and local referral concentration.

d5.bridge: S8 forecasts U.S. mortgage-originations dollars through 2028 and S4 reports title-insurance premium volume, whereas this primitive is five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand for the broader NAICS service basket. I use the near-term mortgage outlook only as directional evidence and extend it cautiously; it likely overstates constant-quality demand because mortgage dollars include house-price effects and does not fully represent cash transactions or commercial work.
d5.rationale: Title and settlement demand is tied to property transactions and mortgage activity. Recent premium growth and the mortgage forecast support a modest central expansion, but cyclicality in rates, inventory, refinancing, and transaction volumes warrants a wide downside range rather than a straight-line extrapolation.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of purchase, refinance, cash, and commercial real-estate transaction counts by state, linked to title-order incidence and agency revenue mix, would materially change this value.
caveats: Premiums and mortgage originations are dollars rather than constant-quality service quantities.
The available mortgage forecast ends before the requested five-year horizon and is not specific to NAICS 541191.

o.rationale: A large majority of remaining demand is expected to require an accountable operator to review title exceptions, coordinate parties, manage funds and closing execution, and take responsibility for errors, even as software handles more intake and record processing. Some standardized transactions may migrate to lender, underwriter, or software-led flows.
o.change_evidence: State-by-state licensing and settlement-responsibility requirements, underwriter delegation agreements, and adoption data for end-to-end digital closings would materially change the operator-required share.
caveats: This is a judgment about future service delivery, not a regulatory measurement of required staffing.
S5 reflects an industry voice and may understate future software substitution.

narrative.executive_view: Independent title abstract and settlement offices perform repeat external transaction services with a document-heavy workflow. The clearest AI use is assistance with intake, search, extraction, record updating, and draft reporting; human accountability for exceptions and closings limits full substitution.
narrative.ai_impact: O*NET describes core work such as examining mortgages, liens, judgments, easements, contracts, and recorded documents; preparing encumbrance reports; and entering data into title records. These activities support AI-assisted retrieval and summarization, while compliance evaluation and resolving title problems still require experienced review.
narrative.value_capture: Title services are often part of a closing-cost comparison and consumers can shop in many cases. Operators may keep some productivity benefit through faster turnaround and lower rework, but price quotes, lender lists, underwriter economics, and referral competition should share a meaningful portion with customers or channel partners.
narrative.firm_availability: Industry announcements document acquisitions of title agencies and abstract firms, so control transfers occur. They do not reveal the denominator of eligible lower-middle-market operators, ownership age, or sales that never become public; availability remains an estimate rather than an observed probability.
narrative.demand_durability: The service is connected to property transfers and mortgage originations, creating durable functional need but pronounced cyclical exposure. ALTA reported 2025 title-insurance premiums of $18.5 billion, up 13.8 percent, while the available mortgage forecast supplies only near-term directional evidence rather than a five-year NAICS demand forecast.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: State-specific practice, underwriter delegation, fraud and wire-security exposure, local referral concentration, and mortgage-cycle swings can alter both implementation and economics. The frozen compensation-to-receipts and firm-count inputs also retain their stated vintage, harmonization, and margin-bridge limitations.
narrative.principal_driver: Document-centric title-search and production work offers a practical target for AI-assisted workflow automation.
narrative.principal_weakness: Real-estate transaction cyclicality and the need for accountable human exception handling constrain both durable demand and full labor substitution.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Title Examiners, Abstractors, and Searchers
sources.supports: Occupation description and core tasks, including examination of title documents, preparation of encumbrance reports, document summarization, record updates, compliance review, and communication to resolve problems.

sources.title: CFPB: Shop for title insurance and other closing services
sources.supports: Title services include title insurance, title search, and often the closing-agent fee; the page says consumers can often shop for closing services and describes variation in closing roles by state.

sources.title: 12 CFR 1024.2 Definitions
sources.supports: The regulation defines title service to include title examination, evaluation, commitment and policy preparation, clearing underwriting objections, administrative processing, and conducting a settlement.

sources.title: ALTA: Title Insurance Premium Volume Increased 13.8% in 2025
sources.supports: ALTA reports $18.5 billion in title-insurance premiums during 2025, a 13.8% increase from 2024, and attributes the increase to mortgage originations.

sources.title: ALTA Industry News 2026
sources.supports: The May 21, 2026 industry-news entry states that algorithms can scan property records and flag data at scale but says experienced human judgment remains important in public-records research; it also notes state compliance guides for all states and the District of Columbia.

sources.title: ALTA: Trusted Land Transfer Acquires New Jersey-based Title Agency
sources.supports: The July 2024 announcement reports Trusted Land Transfer's acquisition of Brennan Title Abstract and notes another acquisition in June 2023.

sources.title: ALTA Industry News 2025
sources.supports: The 2025 news index reports acquisitions including Texas Tradition Title, Delta South Title, Meurer Abstract & Title Company, and LEER Title Services.

sources.title: MBA Mortgage Finance Forecast, April 2026
sources.supports: The forecast table reports total 1-to-4-family mortgage originations of $2.050 trillion for 2025, $2.187 trillion for 2026, $2.199 trillion for 2027, and $2.213 trillion for 2028.
